comment.body:
ZERO GAMMA

if you look at any GEX charts I provide, or at OD heatmaps, you will see multiple zero gamma lines.
Every value that can go from positive to negative and vice versa can be zero too. Charm, vanna, vomma, rho, parmicharm, anything...

The misconception arises from that in naive GEX models the assumption is used that customers sell calls and buy puts. 
So they handle the OI that way, and they will get a gamma profile like this one:

user.name: Alma

comment.body:
SHORT NOTE (as I'm doing classes rn):

Hidden vol selling is going under the hood. The effect just lags yet. 
You can see vol of vol being dumped (white) despite the second top in RV (purple).
Why? - Well, I explained yesterday the reflexiv nature of vol cycles.
This alone telling me without watching at VIX inventories, that customers are selling VIX options, that when they are hedged, it reflexively compresses SPX vomma. (I can also telling without looking into the inventories that these are sold puts by customers more than sold calls, bcs if it would be the other way around then vomma should catch up or at least stagnant as VIX dumps back, as foreshadowed in Sunday. Thats how hedging works...)
This compression of vol-of vol foreshadowing that SPX RV will also be compressed shortly. 
When this happens, you will see systematic reallocation by buying equities, supporting the market up🟢
+ triggers supportive vanna flow into OpEx.

comment.body: what they do is they have a target vol level and they divide it by the current volatility lvl (usually using EWMA for the variance), and this will give them a multiplier to scale the portfolio’s exposure
comment.raw_body: what they do is they have a target vol level and they divide it by the current volatility lvl (usually using EWMA for the variance), and this will give them a multiplier to scale the portfolio’s exposure

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
oooor they can use risk parity, meaning the position size in each asset is inversely proportional to its volatility.
So if an asset’s volatility drops, its weight in the portfolio increases, and vice versa
comment.raw_body:
oooor they can use risk parity, meaning the position size in each asset is inversely proportional to its volatility.
So if an asset’s volatility drops, its weight in the portfolio increases, and vice versa

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
now logically, in the (target vol)/(current vol) model, if the current vol drops below the target vol value, the multiplier will reach above 1
but if the current vol elevates above the target vol value, the multiplier will goe below one.

meaning, that in the first case they will increase the exposure, in the second case they will decrease it
comment.raw_body:

user.name: Alma
user.bio: Privat investor & speculator | Mathematics | Self-made quant | Market researcher | Options and Futures trader

comment.body:
so like:
target vol is 30
current vol is 20
then 30/20 = 1.5
so 1.5x will be the multiplier to scale the portfolio exposure, meaning, they have to add.

if current vol goes above 30, like i.e. to 40, then
30/40 = 0.75
so they will need to decrease their exposure

user.name: Alma

comment.body:
what is VIX? in simply terms: the IV of the next 30 days SPX options. 
if customers sold VIX puts, and this skewed the VIX skew towards shorter (meaning the VIX put IV was sold more than the VIX call IV), then it created a scenario, where VIX dealers had shorter VIX vega (SPX vomma) upside and longer VIX vega (SPX vomma) downside.
VIX started to crush after Sunday gap up (and we discussed here on Sunday night that this gap will be filled, vol will mean revert. remember?) 
and when VIX started to be sold, bcs customers rolled their long 5 delta SPX puts, unwinding some hedges, dealers started to be longer VIX vega to the downside, so they had to hedge that by selling VIX vega, naturally compressing VIX vega (SPX vomma, IV of IV).
comment.raw_body:

comment.body:
the effect of VIX vol dropping is a very hidden effect bcs it is vega on SPX basically.
vega has no short-term effect, but it does have longer-term effect and it works behind the curves.
I will explain, but it is complex. the key here is that this slow vega selling reflexively compresses the overall SPX vol now. 
Soon it will be strong enough to dump rvol too, and then systematics will be triggered to buy into the rally, and new ATH will come as I promised on Monday or Tuesday in the intraday post.
